A 2013 mission concept by Dennis Tito's Inspiration Mars Foundation for a 501-day crewed Mars free-return flyby targeting the 2018 launch window. Two-person crew (a married couple was envisioned for psychological stability) launches on a Falcon Heavy or equivalent into a heliocentric trajectory that swings around Mars without orbit insertion and uses the planet's gravity to slingshot back to Earth. The free-return geometry minimises ∆v — no Mars orbit insertion or trans-Earth burn required — at the cost of a longer transit and a one-shot launch window. The concept did not advance to development; it remains a published reference architecture for crewed cismartian flight.

A 4.6-billion-year-old G-type main-sequence star, halfway through its 10-billion-year life. The Sun rotates differentially — its equator takes 25 days, its poles 35. In about 5 billion years it will exhaust its core hydrogen, expand into a red giant, and engulf the inner planets. The Sun's gravitational influence defines the heliosphere out to roughly 120 AU.

After 11 Soviet failures and 5 American Ranger missions designed only to crash, Luna 9 became the first spacecraft to soft-land on another world. Nine panoramic images returned over 8 minutes settled a long-running scientific debate: the Moon's surface was solid, not a sea of dust deep enough to swallow a lander. The mission lasted 3 days before battery depletion. Within months, every Moon-bound mission afterwards used soft-landing as the baseline.

An uncrewed demonstration of the Starship Mars architecture: launch on Super Heavy, refill in LEO from a tanker variant, then trans-Mars injection of a fully-fuelled Starship and powered descent to the Martian surface. SpaceX's stated target is a 2026-2027 launch window; engineering reality (Starship still working through orbital flight reliability and on-orbit refilling has not been demonstrated) makes 2028 or later more likely. If it succeeds, it inverts the Mars payload economics — from thousands of kg to one hundred tonnes per landing.

Chang'e 5

Chang'e 5 was the first sample return from the Moon since the Soviet Union's Luna 24 in 1976 — a 44-year gap. The architecture: orbiter + lander + ascent vehicle + sample-return capsule, all in one launch. The lander touched down at Mons Rümker, drilled and scooped 1.731 kg of regolith, lifted off, rendezvoused and docked with the orbiter (the first robotic lunar rendezvous), and the capsule returned to Earth. The samples are from a region only 2 billion years old — much younger than any Apollo or Luna sample, rewriting models of lunar volcanism.

China's first Mars mission accomplished what no agency had ever attempted in one launch: orbital insertion, atmospheric entry, soft landing, and rover deployment. The Zhurong rover operated 358 sols at Utopia Planitia, traversing 1.9 km, before entering planned hibernation in May 2022; it has not woken since (likely due to dust accumulation on solar panels). The orbiter remains active, mapping the planet at high resolution and serving as relay for ground assets.

China's first Mars rover. Operated 358 sols, traversed 1.92 km. Returned ground-penetrating radar evidence for buried subsurface layers consistent with ancient catastrophic flooding events. Lost contact during the 2022 Martian winter; CNSA confirmed no further communications.

China's first lunar landing — the first soft landing anywhere on the Moon in 37 years. The Chang'e 3 lander is still alive, making it the longest-surviving lunar lander ever. Yutu drove 114 m before its drive mechanism failed, but its instruments continued returning science data.

Dawn proved ion propulsion at planetary scale: it matched orbits with massive Vesta, left, and spiralled down onto dwarf planet Ceres — the largest object in the asteroid belt. Bright spots in Occator crater became one of the mission's iconic mysteries, later tied to briny eruptions from a deep reservoir. Designed primarily as a Strategic Defense Initiative technology demonstrator, Clementine spent 10 weeks in lunar orbit returning the first systematic global topographic and multispectral map of the Moon. Bistatic radar measurements at the lunar south pole produced the first tantalising hints of water ice — confirmed years later by Lunar Prospector and Chandrayaan-1. The follow-on mission to flyby the asteroid Geographos failed when a thruster malfunction depleted the spacecraft's propellant.

ESA's X-ray multi-mirror mission. 3 telescopes in one spacecraft. Like Chandra, uses a highly elliptical orbit to escape Earth's radiation. Over 1,000 peer-reviewed papers published from its data. Launched the same year as Chandra — two observatories, two continents, the same invisible sky.

ESA's first Mars rover. Originally a Russian-led joint mission planned for 2022; cancelled after the 2022 invasion of Ukraine and rescoped as ESA-led with NASA contributions. Re-targeted for 2028 launch, 2029 arrival. Carries a 2-metre subsurface drill — the deepest yet planned for a Mars rover, designed to reach below the radiation-damaged top metre.

ESA's first interplanetary mission. Carried Beagle 2 to its lost landing. The HRSC stereo camera continues to map Mars at 10 m/pixel; MARSIS subsurface radar discovered evidence of liquid water beneath the south polar cap in 2018.

ESA's first lunar mission, 2003-2006. Used solar-electric (ion) propulsion to spiral out from a geostationary transfer orbit to lunar capture — a 14-month, fuel-efficient transit that pioneered low-thrust trajectories for ESA's later interplanetary missions. Deliberately impacted the lunar surface in 2006.

ESA's first lunar mission. Used solar-electric (ion) propulsion to spiral out from a geostationary transfer orbit to lunar capture — a 14-month, fuel-efficient transit that pioneered low-thrust trajectories for ESA's later interplanetary missions. ESA's first mission to a planet other than Earth. The orbiter has produced the longest continuous record of Mars's atmosphere, mapping water ice deposits, methane plumes, and the planet's three-dimensional weather. Beagle 2, the British-built lander it carried, reached the surface but never deployed solar panels fully — declared lost in 2004, found intact by NASA's Mars Reconnaissance Orbiter in 2015. The orbiter is in its third decade of operation.

ESA's stellar cartographer at L2. Mapping 1.8 billion stars — measuring positions to 7 microarcseconds. Has measured distances to stars 30,000 light-years away with the precision of a human hair's width at 1,000 km distance. The most detailed map of the Milky Way ever made.

ISRO's Mars Orbiter Mission cost $74 million — less than the budget of the film Gravity ($100M), and a fraction of NASA's MAVEN ($671M) which arrived two days earlier. Built in 18 months as a technology demonstrator, Mangalyaan succeeded at every objective: trans-Mars injection, deep-space navigation, Mars orbit insertion, and 8 years of science return. India became the fourth space agency at Mars and the first to succeed on its first try.

ISRO's first interplanetary mission, on a budget of ~$74M — less than the Hollywood film *Gravity*. India became the first Asian nation to reach Mars, and the first country to do so on a maiden interplanetary attempt. Operated 8 years (designed for 6 months) before contact was lost in April 2022.

ISRO's first interplanetary spacecraft. The orbiter delivered the Moon Impact Probe to the lunar surface — the first Indian object on another world — and carried 11 instruments from 6 countries. NASA's Moon Mineralogy Mapper (M3) instrument, hosted on Chandrayaan-1, made the discovery that rewrote textbooks: water molecules are widespread across the lunar surface, not just locked in polar shadowed craters. The mission ended abruptly in August 2009 when ISRO lost communication with the spacecraft.

ISRO's four-stage Polar Satellite Launch Vehicle in its XL configuration with six strap-on solid boosters. Originally designed for sun-synchronous Earth orbits; subsequently used for interplanetary missions including India's first lunar orbiter and first Mars probe. Notable launch-cost efficiency (~$30M per launch) drove the Mangalyaan mission's $74M total budget.

InSight

InSight gave Mars its first stethoscope. Its seismometer, deployed by a robotic arm onto the regolith, recorded over 1,300 marsquakes — including a magnitude-5 in 2022, the largest ever detected on another planet. The data revealed Mars's interior structure: a 1,800-km-radius molten core, a thick crust, and surprising mantle layering. The mole heat-flow experiment failed when the regolith proved too cohesive for its self-hammering design. The mission ended when dust accumulation on solar panels reduced power below operational threshold.

India's first lunar mission, 2008-2009. Mission ended after 312 days when communications were lost; M³ data is still being analysed today.

MAVEN — Mars Atmosphere and Volatile Evolution — answers the question 'where did Mars's air go?' Its elliptical orbit dips into the upper atmosphere, sampling ions and neutrals as the solar wind strips them away. The spacecraft also serves as a critical relay for surface assets, including Curiosity and Perseverance.

SpaceX's long-term crewed Mars architecture as outlined in Elon Musk's 2016, 2017, and 2024 IAC presentations. A Starship lifts off from Earth fully fuelled in low-Earth orbit via a fleet of refilling tanker flights, performs trans-Mars injection, and lands a crew of roughly 10 with cargo and ISRU equipment on the Martian surface. Return-leg propellant (methane + liquid oxygen) is manufactured on Mars from atmospheric CO₂ and subsurface water ice via the Sabatier process, enabling the same Starship to lift off again, perform trans-Earth injection, and return the crew. Round-trip duration depends on the launch window and surface stay; the illustrative timeline shown here is ~2.5 years door-to-door (213 days outbound, ~500-day surface stay, return on the next favourable window). The architecture is conceptual — date and parameters subject to substantial revision pending Starship maturation and ISRU validation.

The Artemis program's first crewed flight, and the spark that became Orrery. NASA's SLS Block 1 launched an Orion spacecraft with four astronauts — Reid Wiseman (CDR), Victor Glover (PLT), Christina Koch (MS), and CSA's Jeremy Hansen (MS) — onto a hybrid free-return trajectory that swung them around the Moon's far side at roughly 9,200 km altitude and returned them to Earth without any lunar-orbit insertion burn. A 10-day mission ending with Pacific splashdown. Qualified Orion's life-support, navigation, and re-entry systems for crewed flight ahead of the Artemis III surface landing.

The Emirates Mars Mission — Al Amal, 'Hope' — was conceived in 2014 to mark the 50th anniversary of UAE federation in 2021. The MBRSC team partnered with the University of Colorado, Arizona State, and UC Berkeley to design and integrate the spacecraft, but built it themselves. The science orbit (1× day duration, 27,000 km × 43,000 km) covers all longitudes and times of day, capturing weather patterns no other Mars orbiter can resolve. The mission succeeded on the first attempt, making the UAE the fifth agency at Mars.
The Functional Cargo Block launched first—propulsion, power, and attitude functions that let Unity dock days later. NASA funded Russian construction during the programme’s hardest transition years.
The ISS orbits at 408 km. You could drive there in 4 hours at motorway speed. It feels impossibly far — it is impossibly close.
The Moon is 384,400 km away — almost 1,000 times farther than the ISS. On this logarithmic map it looks close. In reality, no human has been there since 1972.

The Soviet Union's audacious double-mission to Mars in 1971 — Mars 2 and Mars 3 launched 9 days apart. Mars 2's lander crashed; Mars 3's lander achieved the first soft-landing on Mars but transmitted for only ~110 seconds before signal loss, possibly due to the global dust storm raging at the time. The orbiter operated for 8 months, returning hundreds of images and atmospheric measurements. Despite the brief surface mission, Mars 3 demonstrated that the descent and landing problem on Mars was solvable.

The Soviet Union's final lunar mission and the last uncrewed lunar landing until China's Chang'e 3 in 2013 — a gap of 37 years. The drilled core extracted regolith from 2 m depth and returned 170 g to Earth. Among the samples: minerals containing trace water, foreshadowing the discovery of substantial lunar water ice deposits 30 years later. Luna 24 also marked the closure of the Soviet lunar programme; resources shifted to Mars and Venus.
The Sun
The Sun (NASA Solar System Exploration)

The Sun contains 99.86% of all mass in the solar system. Its gravity holds everything — from Mercury to the Oort Cloud — in orbit. Every second, it fuses 600 million tonnes of hydrogen into helium, releasing the energy that has sustained life on Earth for 4.5 billion years.
The U.S. segment’s crew airlock—where suited astronauts step into vacuum without opening a docking port on a crew vehicle.
The first U.S.-built connecting node—six berthing ports that turned scattered elements into a wiring-linked station instead of separate cylinders.

The first crewed lunar landing in over half a century. Architecture: NASA's SLS Block 1 launches Orion with 4 crew to a Near-Rectilinear Halo Orbit (NRHO) around the Moon. Pre-positioned at NRHO is a SpaceX Starship Human Landing System (HLS), refilled in low-Earth orbit by tankers before its trans-lunar injection. The Orion crew transfers to HLS; 2 astronauts descend to the lunar south pole region for ~6.5 days of surface ops while 2 remain in NRHO. After surface operations, HLS launches back to NRHO for transfer to Orion and Earth return. Will land the first woman and first person of colour on the Moon.

The first crewed lunar landing since Apollo 17 in 1972 — a 55-year gap. Targets the south pole for its water ice deposits, which may enable refuelling for future deep space missions. Will land the first woman and first person of colour on the Moon. If it happens on schedule, it begins the sustained human presence that leads to Mars.

The first humans on the Moon. Watched live by ~600 million people — a fifth of humanity. The laser retroreflector placed by Armstrong and Aldrin still receives pulses today, measuring the Earth-Moon distance to millimetre precision. Footprints in the airless regolith will survive unchanged for a million years.

The first remote-controlled rover to operate on another world. Drove 10.5 km over 322 days, photographing over 20,000 images. Lost to history until 2010, when the Lunar Reconnaissance Orbiter spotted it from orbit — still sitting exactly where it stopped.

The first spacecraft to achieve a soft landing on the Moon and transmit photographs from the surface. Nine panoramic images ended the debate about whether landers would sink into deep lunar dust. A defining moment — the Moon could be reached.

The first spacecraft to land on the Moon's far side — technically and operationally the hardest lunar landing ever attempted. Required a relay satellite (Queqiao) at L2 since the far side has no direct radio contact with Earth. Yutu-2 has driven 1.9+ km, still active. The far side had never been visited.

The first wheels on the Moon — driven from a control room in Crimea. Lunokhod 1 was a 322 kg pressurised tub mounted on eight independently-driven wheels, controlled by a five-person team in real time across the 2.5-second light-speed delay. It drove 10.5 km over 11 lunar days (322 Earth days), photographed 20,000+ scenes, and operated long past its 90-day design lifetime. Lost to history in the 1970s, located again in 2010 when NASA's Lunar Reconnaissance Orbiter spotted it sitting where it stopped.

The flagship mission that defined modern Mars exploration. Curiosity is car-sized, plutonium-powered, and has driven 32+ km up Mount Sharp since landing in Gale Crater in 2012. Its instruments confirmed Mars once had habitable conditions — flowing water, complex organics, the chemistry life would need. The 'seven minutes of terror' EDL sequence, ending with a sky-crane lowering the rover on cables, is now NASA's standard for delivering large payloads to Mars surface.
The forward utility node that bridged Destiny to European and Japanese labs—think crowded corridor turned international interchange.

The geostationary ring. At exactly 35,786 km, orbital period equals Earth's rotation — satellites appear fixed in the sky. GOES weather satellites, Meteosat, thousands of communications satellites. The specific altitude where physics and utility perfectly align.
The high inclination (64.8°) means GLONASS provides better coverage of Russia and the Arctic — geography shaped the orbital design.
The highest eccentricity of the inner planets (e=0.093). Mars is 25% farther from Earth at aphelion vs perihelion — this is why launch windows matter so much.

The last Soviet lunar mission — and the last uncrewed Moon landing until China's Chang'e 3 in 2013: a gap of 37 years. Returned 170 g of samples including minerals containing water, foreshadowing the later discovery of lunar water ice.

The last time humans visited another world — over half a century ago. Cernan and Schmitt spent 75 hours on the surface, drove the LRV 35.7 km, and brought back 110.5 kg of samples. Schmitt was the only trained scientist (geologist) ever to walk on the Moon, and his findings — particularly the orange volcanic glass at Shorty Crater — reshaped lunar geology. Cernan's last words before climbing the ladder for the final time: 'We leave as we came, and God willing, we shall return.' Artemis III is supposed to make good on that promise.

The last time humans visited the Moon. Gene Cernan was the last person to walk on its surface. Before climbing the ladder, he said: 'We leave as we came, and God willing, we shall return.' Harrison Schmitt, the only trained geologist on the Moon, collected samples that reshaped our understanding of lunar geology.
The launcher that put every Mars Express, Cluster, and ESA-Roscosmos joint mission on its way

The least dense planet — it would float on water. The ring system spans 282,000 km but is only metres thick. The 26.73° tilt means Earth sees the rings at different angles across Saturn's 29-year orbit.

The longest continuously-active spacecraft in orbit around any planet other than Earth. Detected vast subsurface water-ice deposits in 2002 (a discovery that drove the Phoenix and Curiosity targeting). Still active in 2026 as a relay node for surface assets.

The longest-running active lunar orbital mission. LROC's narrow-angle camera resolves objects ≥50 cm — sharp enough to image every Apollo descent stage, both Lunokhod rover tracks, and every recent landed asset. Built the topographic and imagery base maps used by every recent Moon-landing mission. Currently mapping permanently-shadowed craters for Artemis south-pole site selection.

The lunar far side has no direct radio line of sight to Earth. To land a spacecraft there, China first launched the Queqiao relay satellite to a halo orbit around Earth-Moon Lagrange point L2 — a vantage point that sees both the far side and Earth simultaneously. With that infrastructure in place, Chang'e 4 became the first spacecraft to soft-land on the far side. The Yutu-2 rover has been operating since January 2019 and has driven 1.9+ km, the longest traverse by any lunar rover.
The massive Russian multipurpose lab that arrived decades into assembly—science volume plus propellant transfer ambitions.

The mission that ended dreams of a habitable Mars. Mariner 4's 22 grainy images, taken during a 25-minute closest approach at 9,846 km altitude, showed a cratered surface resembling the Moon — no canals, no vegetation, no obvious life. The atmosphere was 100× thinner than expected. The discovery reshaped Mars in the public imagination from sister-Earth to dead world. Tracking the spacecraft also produced the first measurement of the solar wind's structure between planets.

The mission that fulfilled Kennedy's 1961 commitment to put a man on the Moon by the end of the decade. Armstrong and Aldrin spent 21 hours 36 minutes on the lunar surface — 2.5 hours of EVA, 21.55 kg of samples, and a single commemorative plaque. The laser retroreflector they installed at Mare Tranquillitatis still receives pulses today, measuring the Earth-Moon distance to millimetre precision. The mission was watched live by ~600 million people, one fifth of humanity at the time.

The mission that gave us our first sustained look at the Martian surface. Viking 1's lander operated for 6 years 4 months at Chryse Planitia — running biology experiments designed to detect life (results ambiguous; one experiment produced a positive signal still debated decades later), measuring weather, returning panoramic images, and analysing soil composition. The orbiter mapped 97% of Mars at 100m resolution and helped scout landing sites for missions still decades away.

The mission that proved cheap interplanetary missions could work. Pathfinder used a novel airbag landing system — bouncing dozens of times across the surface before coming to rest — and delivered Sojourner, the first rover to operate on another planet. Sojourner traversed 100 m, examined a dozen rocks, and worked far past its 7-day design lifetime. Pathfinder operated for 2 months 27 days; the mission's success revived NASA's interplanetary programme after the failures of the 1990s.

The only mission to land in the lunar highlands. Charles Duke left a photo of his family sealed in a bag on the lunar surface. On the back he wrote: 'This is the family of Astronaut Duke from Planet Earth. Landed on the Moon, April 1972.' It will outlast civilisation.

The precision landing — Conrad touched down within 183 m of the uncrewed Surveyor 3 probe (1967). They retrieved pieces of it for return to Earth, showing spacecraft survive years on the Moon undamaged by radiation or micrometeorites. A proof-of-concept for future rendezvous missions.
The primary U.S. laboratory—a shirtsleeve workspace where rack-mounted experiments replaced one-off shuttle middeck demos.
The smallest planet. Its highly elliptical orbit (e=0.206) makes it accelerate noticeably near perihelion. Despite being closest to the Sun, Venus is hotter.
The station’s long yellow crane—walking hand-over-hand along the truss to capture cargo vehicles without astronaut wrestle matches.

The telescope that changed how humanity sees the universe. Launched 1990 with a flawed mirror — corrected by Space Shuttle astronauts in 1993. Has since observed 1.4 million celestial objects. Operating 34+ years.

The twin that kept going when budgets tightened, Voyager 2 carried the Grand Tour from Jupiter and Saturn out to Uranus and Neptune — still the only close-up images we have of the ice giants. Its Neptune encounter in 1989 revealed the Great Dark Spot and Triton's geysers. Today both Voyagers sail in interstellar space with a golden record as humanity's calling card. Orrery lists Neptune as the catalogue destination; the real path relied on multiple gravity assists.

Three-core Falcon 9 derivative. The two side boosters return to launch sites or droneships; the centre core lands at sea or is expended for maximum payload. Demonstrated in 2018 with the launch of a Tesla Roadster on a heliocentric trajectory crossing the orbit of Mars.

Three-stage hypergolic launcher with four liquid strap-ons, in service since 1996. Standard configuration for GTO commercial launches and most lunar missions: Chang'e 1 (2007), Chang'e 2 (2010), Chang'e 3 (2013) and Chang'e 4 (2018). Retired from lunar duty when the larger Long March 5 took over for sample-return missions (Chang'e 5/6) and Tianwen-1.

Two-stage launcher in two configurations: Ariane 62 with two solid boosters and Ariane 64 with four. The Vinci-powered cryogenic upper stage is restartable, enabling complex multi-orbit deployments and direct interplanetary injection. Inaugural flight July 2024.

Two-stage stainless-steel vehicle with a Super Heavy booster (33 Raptor engines) and a Starship upper stage (6 Raptors). Architected around orbital propellant transfer: a tanker variant refills Starship in LEO, enabling ~100 t to Mars surface — orders of magnitude more than any prior launcher. Orbital flight programme began 2023.

UK-led astrobiology lander aboard ESA's Mars Express. Touched down on Christmas Day 2003 but never made contact. Wreckage was located in 2015 by NASA's MRO HiRISE camera — the lander had reached the surface intact, but two of its four solar panels failed to deploy, blocking its radio antenna. A 12-year mystery solved by orbital imagery.

ULA Atlas V configured with a 5-metre fairing, four solid rocket boosters, and a single-engine Centaur upper stage. Launched Curiosity to Mars in November 2011. Retired in 2024 in favour of the Vulcan Centaur.

Unity (Node 1)

Until SLIM, lunar landings had a kilometre-scale targeting accuracy. JAXA's Smart Lander demonstrated 100 m accuracy — the precision required for crewed missions to dock with pre-positioned cargo, for sample-return ascent vehicles to find their orbital partners, and for any future architecture that depends on landing at specific scientifically valuable sites. SLIM also pioneered low-energy ballistic transfer to the Moon, taking 135 days to arrive but using less propellant than direct trajectories. A demonstration of the patience interplanetary missions can afford when timeline isn't the binding constraint.
